title: Ubuntu Server & CLI Basics
summary: A beginner-friendly path for understanding your Ubuntu Server, terminal commands, Samba shares, and Tailscale access.

skills.title: Ubuntu Server Orientation
skills.summary: How does a "server" work compared to a "Point & Click" graphics based computer?

intro:
Your Ubuntu server is now acting like a small appliance in your home.

It does not need a normal desktop screen like Windows. If you set up your server on a laptop you log-in directly on it, but most of the time, you will control it on your PC through Command Prompt / Powershell Terminal.  Otherwise it's operating "headless", executing all the actions & programs you've set up without constant input. The terminal is not “the server.” The terminal is just a text-based control panel.

You talk to your server using a scripting language callled 'BASH' (Bourne Again SHell). Instead of clicking icons like you do in a graphical interface, you type textual instructions into Bash to navigate files, run applications, and configure system settings. Bash 'prints' results on the screen for you.
CORE IDENTITY ELEMENTS: First thing first, lets explore the various identity and location commands.

commands.purpose: Show me this computer’s network name. You set this during Ubuntu install.
commands.outcome: Prints server name you designated, This name may sometimes appear in your router, on your network, or in connection instructions.

commands.purpose: An existential command, "What is the username I'm logged in as"?
commands.outcome: Example : User1

commands.purpose: means: “Show me the folder I am currently standing in". PRINT in this case means 'print on screen'.
commands.outcome:
Prints a file path location:
example  '/home/User1'

commands.purpose: lists the files and folders in your current location. If you are in /home/User1
commands.outcome:
'ls' shows what is inside:
/User1
Documents
Downloads
Samwizard_Data

commands.purpose: “Show me the network addresses assigned to this server.”
commands.outcome: Your server may show several network sections. The one you usually care about looks something like:  192.168.1.50

commands.purpose: Show me how much disk space is used and free, in a human-readable format.
commands.outcome:
Ex:
Filesystem      Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda2       100G   25G   75G  25% /
/dev/sdb1       2.0T  500G  1.5T  25% /mnt/storage

commands.purpose: A superuser is an administrator-level account with permission to change important parts of the system. So sudo means: “Run this command with administrator power.”
commands.outcome: When you put 'sudo' at the start of a command (ex: sudo apt upgrade) you will be asked for your password to confirm you have the authority to take this action.

lesson.exercise:
Run these commands one by one to reveal your server Identity and Location: 
whoami
hostname
ip address
pwd
df -h
lesson.paste_prompt: Paste your results to keep a snapshot of how it all started right here if you need to come back and reference it.

skills.title: Remote Control / Terminal Access
skills.summary: Logging Into Your Server from Another Computer: How to open a terminal and safely connect to their Ubuntu server using SSH.

intro:
SSH means 'Secure Shell' , Plain-English meaning:
- SSH lets you control your Ubuntu server from another computer using a secure text connection. If you set up your server on an old laptop, you don't HAVE To do this, but it's very handy to SSH , especially if you're trying something new and need to be able to Copy/Paste commands in.

the user can open:

- Command Prompt
- PowerShell
- Windows Terminal
- macOS Terminal
- another Linux terminal

The user needs three things:

- The server’s username
- server’s IP address
- server account’s password

commands.purpose: remote login just as if you were working AT your server
commands.outcome:
The first time the user connects, they may see something like:
"Are you sure you want to continue connecting? Y/N"

- YES means: “I recognize this server and want to save this connection as trusted.”

commands.purpose: easier to remember than the ip address
commands.outcome: Most times on your local network (if your router supports local hostnames) you can use the name of your server instead of the ip address.

lesson.exercise: if you haven't already,  SSH into your server and use that terminal to continue on with the course.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the two short outputs here.

skills.title: Users & Permissions
skills.summary: Your Ubuntu user name is not just a login. It is the main administrator account for the entire server. In this segment we'll discuss how to create, manage, and remove other users, including the difference between Ubuntu system users and Samba file-sharing users.

intro:
Right now you should have an SSH connection to your server with your main login, 
This account can:

Install software
Change server settings
Create and delete users
Change passwords
Start and stop services
Modify shared folders
Change permissions
Break things if used carelessly**

Your admin user is the owner of the server’s steering wheel. This is why Ubuntu often asks for your password before important changes. It is making sure the person making the change is allowed to do it.

Most beginners may only need one user at first. But later, you might create another Ubuntu user if:
-Someone else needs access to the server
-You want a separate non-admin account
-A service needs its own account
-You want different people to have different file permissions
-You want a Samba user for a specific person
**Beginner rule:**
Do not give every user admin power. Most users should not be in the sudo group.

commands.purpose: A superuser is an administrator-level user with permission to change important parts of the system.
commands.outcome: commands with SUDO in front of it will execute with admin power.  If a particular command NEEDS admin power to execute, and you haven't included SUDO first, it will warn you that action can't not be completed without admin permission.

commands.origin: “Show me the permission groups my current user belongs to.”
commands.purpose: Making sure all the necessary permissions are assigned to the user
commands.outcome:
sam adm cdrom sudo dip plugdev

means: The user sam belongs to several permission groups, including the sudo group.

commands.origin: “Using administrator permission, create a new Ubuntu system user named alex.”
commands.purpose: Create the user first, then set permissions or assign to groups after.
commands.outcome: user 'alex' would be created and Ubuntu will ask you to set a password after.

commands.purpose: “Using administrator permission, modify the user named alex by adding them to the administrator group.”
commands.outcome: alex now has A LOT of power, make sure you trust alex. (we don't)

lesson.exercise: create a new user called 'watchman', don't assign any elevated permissions. just keep it handy in case you want to log in and monitor your system without making any changes.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the hostname and first IP address here.

skills.title: Files, Folders, and Paths
skills.summary: Linux/Ubuntu organizes files, how to move around safely, and how to understand paths without treating the file system like a mystery maze.

intro:
Linux uses one big folder tree that starts at /. Drives, user folders, services, and system files all appear somewhere under that root.
Windows often feels like this:
C:\
D:\
E:\
Linux looks like this:
root/           (The top of the Ubuntu file system)
├── home (users folders)
├── etc     (system & config files)
├── mnt   (extra drives mounted)
├── srv      (server shared drives)
├── var      (System Logs)
└── usr     (Installed system programs and resources)

Example: /home/samuser  “The terminal is currently standing inside the samuser’s home folder.”

SamWizard uses folders under /srv because /srv is commonly used for data served to other computers.
Important warning:

Beginners should mostly work inside /home, /mnt, or SamWizard-created folders. Avoid editing /etc unless SamWizard or a trusted guide specifically tells you to.

commands.purpose: List everything in this folder, using a long detailed view, including hidden files.
commands.outcome:
Hidden files in Ubuntu often start with a dot:
.bashrc
.profile
.config

commands.purpose: “Move the terminal into another folder.”
commands.outcome:
'cd /home/user/notes' to do work INSIDE your notes folder 

'cd ~'  will take you straight to your home folder (~ = home)

commands.purpose: Displays a hierarchical, tree-like overview of all available storage drives and their partitions. It is highly useful for identifying device names (e.g., sda, nvme0n1), understanding disk relationships, and checking where partitions are mounted.  ADD " -f" for file system info included. ADD " -d" to hide partitions and show just the storage devices.
commands.outcome:
NAME        MAJ:MIN RM   SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINT
sda           8:0    0 465.8G  0 disk 
├─sda1        8:1    0   512M  0 part /boot/efi
├─sda2        8:2    0     4G  0 part [SWAP]
└─sda3        8:3    0 461.3G  0 part /
nvme0n1     259:0    0 931.5G  0 disk 
└─nvme0n1p1 259:1    0 931.5G  0 disk /mnt/storage

lesson.exercise: Run 'lsblk -f' and identify your SAMBA share drive with it's UUID number, copy & paste it below for future reference.

skills.title: UFW Firewall Basics
skills.summary: The Server’s Front Door: what UFW is, why a firewall matters, and how to safely allow the services they actually need: SSH, Samba, and possibly Tailscale-related access.

intro:
UFW means: Uncomplicated Firewall

Plain-English meaning: UFW is Ubuntu’s beginner-friendly firewall tool. It controls what network traffic is allowed to reach your server.

A firewall is like a locked front desk for the server.
It decides:
- What is allowed in
- What is blocked
- Which services other computers can reach

Main idea:
Your server should only leave open the doors it actually needs.

A fresh Ubuntu server may run services like:
-SSH remote login
-Samba file sharing
-Tailscale private networking
-Web dashboards
-Future apps like Nextcloud, Cockpit, or Home Assistant

Each service may listen on a port. A port is like a numbered doorway into the server.

Service	Common Port	             Meaning
SSH	                22	                     Remote terminal access
Samba	       445	                     Windows file sharing
HTTP	        80	                     Normal web traffic
HTTPS	       443                        Secure web traffic

If the user is connected through SSH, they must allow SSH before turning the firewall on.
Otherwise, they may lock themselves out of the server.

Important rule:
Before enabling UFW, make sure SSH is allowed.

**All of this is likely moot for you at the moment, if you already installed SAMBA & TAILSCALE, as those install wizards offered to turn on UFW for you during those processes. **

commands.origin: “Using administrator permission, show me the current firewall status.”
commands.purpose: Is your firewall even on?
commands.outcome: Status: inactive (nope, it's not on)

commands.origin: The detailed version of the previous command
commands.purpose: “Using administrator permission, show detailed firewall status and rules.”
commands.outcome:
Status: active

To                 Action      From
--                 ------      ----
OpenSSH            ALLOW       Anywhere
Samba              ALLOW       Anywhere

commands.purpose: Ubuntu may warn that enabling the firewall could disrupt existing SSH connections.
commands.outcome: Firewall system turned on, now you can add services allowed to access ports.

commands.purpose: This allows other computers on the network to reach the shared folders.
commands.outcome: If you couldn't access your SAMBA share, this might have been why. Now you can.

lesson.exercise: Run the detailed ufw status check, make sure the services you need accessible , are.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the final ufw status output.

skills.title: Logs & Troubleshooting
skills.summary: You will learn how to check Ubuntu logs, search through them, and use those clues when Samba, or other services are not behaving correctly.

intro:
Logs are the server’s notes about what happened.
They can help answer questions like:

-Did Samba start correctly?
-Did someone connect to a share?
-Did Tailscale lose connection?
-Did a service crash?
-Did the server restart?
-Did Ubuntu block or reject something?

Logs do not fix the problem by themselves. They help you find where the problem started.

commands.purpose: Ubuntu uses a logging system called the journal.
commands.outcome:
Dec 02 13:39:41 systemd[1]: Started session-66.scope
Dec 02 13:40:10 systemd[1]: Starting sysstat-collect.service
Dec 02 13:40:10 systemd[1]: sysstat-collect.service: Deactivated

commands.origin: System Log Journal for unit SAMBA
commands.purpose: Check Logs for One Service
commands.outcome:
Dec 02 21:32:03  starting smbd.service
Dec 04 05:38:29  Stopping smbd.service

commands.purpose: If you've been following along.....use a '--since' modifier to prevent the results from starting the day your server or that service was created.
commands.outcome:
OTHERS: 
journalctl --since "1 hour ago"
journalctl --since "30 minutes ago"
journalctl -u ssh --since today

lesson.exercise: Inspect the logs for your last SSH login.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the error line you saw.

skills.title: Manage SAMBA
skills.summary: Learn how Samba fits into Ubuntu, how Samba users are different from Ubuntu users, how to check whether Samba is running, and what has to happen before a new drive can become a Samba share.

intro:
Samba is the service that lets your Ubuntu server share folders with Windows, macOS, and Linux computers. Samba does not create magic storage. It shares real folders that already exist on your Ubuntu server.

Your server has Ubuntu system users.
These users can log into the server, own files, and run commands. A Samba user usually starts as a real Ubuntu user, but Samba keeps its own separate password.

Ubuntu system user--->Logging into the server, owning files, using SSH
Samba user--->Ubuntu user with access to shared folders from Windows/macOS/Linux

Your main actions with SAMBA after install will be ADDING NEW DRIVES or ADDING NEW USERS.   
-New Drives: Get yourself a powered USB Hub and start racking up the storage if you want, you can set up a ROBOCOPY script on windows to back up your PC periodically. 
-New Users: If you have other folks in your home you trust (we don't) you can give them access to JUST their drive.  WARNING: Add a new user and SamWizard will not be able to help you mount a new drive... SamWizard is for novices & noobs... not skilled Linux Wizards.

commands.purpose: “Ask Ubuntu whether the main Samba file-sharing service is running.”
commands.outcome: active (running)

commands.purpose: After most changes to SAMBA , a restart of that system will be required.
commands.outcome: Samba will restart

commands.purpose: “Check Samba’s configuration file and show the active Samba settings in a simplified form.”
commands.outcome:
ex:
[Backups]
[Media]
[Storage]

commands.purpose: “Using administrator permission, list the users in Samba’s password database.”
commands.outcome: This shows Samba users, not every Ubuntu system user.

commands.purpose: alex is an existing Ubuntu user.... but now alex is getting a SAMBA password, so he can use SAMBA
commands.outcome: alex is now a SAMBA user

lesson.exercise: Run hostname -I and compare it with the address SamWizard shows in setup screens.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the first local IP address.

skills.title: Drives And Mounts
skills.summary: You will learn how Ubuntu sees drives, why Linux “mounts” drives into folders, how to inspect drives safely, and how to mount a drive.

intro:
Ubuntu has one big folder tree that starts at:   /
That top folder is called the root of the filesystem.

When Ubuntu uses a drive, it attaches that drive to a folder.
Example:
/mnt/storage

That means:  “The drive is available through the folder /mnt/storage.”

Main idea:  In Linux, drives do not become D: or E:. They become folders.

Important Vocabulary:

- Drive=	A physical or virtual storage device, like an HDD, SSD, USB drive, or NVMe drive
- Partition=	A section of a drive that can hold a filesystem
- Filesystem=	The format used to organize files on a partition, such as ext4, exFAT, or NTFS
- Mount=   	Attach a drive or partition to a folder
- Mount point= 	The folder where the drive appears
- UUID=     	A unique ID for a partition
- /etc/fstab= 	The file that tells Ubuntu what drives to mount automatically at startup

commands.purpose: Shows disks, partitions, sizes, and mount points.
commands.outcome:
Prints a tree of storage devices.

use 'lsblk -f' for File System Data included
use 'lsblk -d' to hide partitions and just show full drives

commands.purpose: use to show ALL mounted file systems -OR- try a more narrow location like,  'findmnt /mnt/mystuff
commands.outcome: Prints target folders and source devices.

commands.purpose:
Using administrator permission, create the folder /mnt/mystuff so a drive can be attached there next.' (Drives get attached to folders) 
-----------------------
-p = "Create parent folders if needed and do not complain if the folder already exists"
commands.outcome:
It creates a folder you'll 'mount' your drive to. (like assigning a mailbox to someone in an apartment building. 

The next step will be a multi-part process to assign your physical drive to that mounting folder so it shows up there every time you boot up.

commands.origin: File System Table , a text document storing file system configuration details.
commands.purpose: This command opens a text file in your terminal. You will edit this file so your server knows that YOUR STORAGE DEVICE should be mounted to /mnt/mystuff  every time it boots.  BUT FIRST , collect this information:
commands.outcome:
Run 'lsblk -f' and look for: 
UUID : a static id number for your drive device
FSType:  Filesystem format ( ie: NTFS, exfat )
- - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - 
String it all together like this: 
"UUID=a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-111122223333 /mnt/mystuff ntfs defaults,nofail 0 2"

- Paste it at the bottom of the /fstab. 
- Press "CTL+O"  then ENTER to save
- Press "CTL+X" to exit. 

The file system table (fstab) will now tell your server WHERE to put that specific device every time you boot up.

commands.purpose: Now that you've updated the file system table with you mounting instructions, this will test your update by telling ubuntu to mount everything listed in /etc/fstab that is not already mounted.
commands.outcome:
NAME                      MAJ:MIN RM   SIZE RO TYPE MOUNTPOINTS
sda                         8:0    0 476.9G  0 disk
└─sda1                      8:1    0 476.9G  0 part /mnt/SSK-Portable
sdb                         8:16   0   4.5T  0 disk
├─sdb1                      8:17   0    16M  0 part
└─sdb2                      8:18   0   4.5T  0 part /mnt/mystuff

lesson.exercise: When you're ready, find a reason to mount a second drive to your server, and give it a whirl.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste one drive line that includes a mount point.

skills.title: Mount New SAMBA Drive
skills.summary: You will take a mounted drive Ubuntu can already see and turn that drive into a Samba share that other computers can access.

intro:
A drive must already be mounted before Samba can share it.
Example mounted drive:
/mnt/mystuff

How SAMBA will Share it: 
\\100.0.0.0\mystuff
Follow the below steps to add a new drive to your SAMBA Share setup.

commands.origin: Drives and free space -  human readable
commands.purpose: Confirm the Drive you're adding is actually mounted.
commands.outcome:
Filesystem         Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda1          477G  102G  376G  22% /mnt/myfirstdrive
/dev/sdb2          4.6T  1.1T  3.5T  24% /mnt/mystuff

commands.origin: Change ownership of everything in the /mnt/mystuff folder
commands.purpose: Give the right user ownership - “Make the Ubuntu user alex the owner of the shared folder and (-R,  everything inside it.)”
commands.outcome: Prints uid, gid, and groups.

commands.origin: Make user alex a SAMBA password
commands.purpose: Ensure the user for /mnt/mystuff actually is a SAMBA user
commands.outcome: Asked to confirm password or told it already exists.

commands.origin: Opens the Samba Configuration Text file
commands.purpose:
Add your new share drive to the SAMBA CONFIGURATION file. 
Paste the below config info at the bottom of the text file.
commands.outcome:
[MyStuff]
   path = /mnt/mystuff
   browseable = yes
   read only = no
   valid users = alex
-----------------------
'CTL + O'  then ENTER to save changes
'CTL + X'    to exit

lesson.exercise: Open 'WIN + R' and type in "   \\Servier-ip-address\MyStuff  "  |  You should be prompted to enter login/pswd ,  use alex's login.  Then brag about your wizardry in the block below.
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the permission letters from the first column.

skills.title: System Updates - Maintenance
skills.summary: You will learn how to safely update Ubuntu Server, remove old unused packages, reboot when needed, and confirm that Samba and Tailscale are still working afterward.

intro:
Your Ubuntu server needs regular maintenance.
Updates help with:
-Security fixes
-Bug fixes
-Software improvements
-Samba stability
-Tailscale reliability
-General server health

Main idea:
Updating your server is normal maintenance, not a special emergency task.
A good beginner routine is:
-Update weekly or every couple of weeks.
-Reboot when Ubuntu says a reboot is needed.
-Check Samba and Tailscale afterward.

The best part is you can do this all remotely, via SSH. If all you're using this server for is SAMBA share , this will be generally the ONLY interaction you have with your server regularly. Super Simple. And it's GLOBAL, so if you do have other services installed, it updates everything, not just UBUNTU, but SAMBA , TAILSCALE & anything else you have installed.

commands.origin: Tells Ubuntu Package Manager to look for updates
commands.purpose: “Using administrator permission, ask Ubuntu to refresh its list of available software updates.” Not to actually update anything, just verify what is AVAILABLE to update.
commands.outcome:
Fetched 442 kB in 1s (295 kB/s)
1 package can be upgraded. Run 'apt list --upgradable' to see it.

commands.purpose: “Using administrator permission, install the available safe updates for software already installed on this server.”  It may ask you a Y/N confirming you want to actually preform the updates.
commands.outcome:
Upgrading: 1, Installing: 0, Removing: 0, Not Upgrading: 0
  Download size: 42.7 kB
  Space needed: 1024 B / 1023 GB available
-------------
... or something like that.

commands.origin: remove old packages we don't need anymore.
commands.purpose:
“Using administrator permission, remove old software packages that Ubuntu no longer needs.”This often removes old helper packages or old Linux kernel components after updates.

Beginner rule:
'autoremove' is usually safe, but you should still read the summary before saying yes.
commands.outcome: List of available packages to be removed.  Say Y/N

commands.purpose:
Sometimes after an update, you'll need to reboot the whole server. *** System restart required ***
This is where you'll feel most like an IT guy.... if you have other users on your network, let them know before you flip the switch, they could loose some data if they're in the middle of a transfer.
commands.outcome: System restarts.

commands.purpose: It is Ubuntu’s main command for how you check, start, stop, restart, and enable the background services that make your server useful. A service is a background program that runs without you manually opening it, like: -Samba file sharing - OR - SSH remote login.
commands.outcome:
COMMAND : systemctl status smbd

Means:
“Show me whether the Samba service is running.”

lesson.exercise: Run the Update / Upgrade & Reboot cycle....  get use to the feel of that much power in your hands.

skills.title: FINAL EXAM  Shell Script
skills.summary: One last task to display your wizardry... we'll write a script to let you call up a Quick Reference Guide YOU made for YOU!

intro:
Congratulations! You've completed the SamWizard Academy Skill Tree, all the tools you'll need to manage your SAMBA share cloud server, AND MORE!!!   

Instead of a diploma.... we're going to write a script... a file that contains a list of commands for Ubuntu to execute at your will, with a single command.... otherwise known in some circles as....

CASTING A SPELL !

Presumably this whole time you've been making use of the NOTE PAD here in the Academy... we'll let's use that to make a little cheat sheet, a list of commands YOU want to be able to recall anytime you log into your server. Or if you think "cheat sheet' is a bit dodgy... we'll call it a Quick Reference guide.  Either way... get your list in order... and we'll proceed.

commands.origin: Make folder in my home directory called "SW-notes"
commands.purpose: Your notes the spell is going to call have to live somewhere,  this is the folder we'll keep it in.
commands.outcome:
RUN: 
cd ~
ls -la
(Confirm your new notes folder is in your home directory)

commands.origin: Open new txt file in editor, called "commands.txt"
commands.purpose: This is the actual file you'll put all your commands and notes you want to recall later.
commands.outcome:
Nano text editor will open with a blank page, copy / paste all the notes you want to keep here. 
----------------------
CTL + O  and ENTER = SAVE
CTL + X  = Exit

commands.origin: Show me my txt file on screen
commands.purpose: Test your file. This will present the commands.txt file as you wrote it on screen, not just a string of text. The 'print' command would just show a string of text.
commands.outcome: Your Commands.txt doc should appear

commands.origin: Make a folder where my personal commands live
commands.purpose: There are many commands in linux, but this will be a list of YOUR custom commands.
commands.outcome: Folder Created.

commands.origin: open editor for a new command called samnotes
commands.purpose: This is going to open the nano text editor so you can create the config file (The script) that tells Ubuntu what to do when you type the command 'samnotes' into the terminal. (your spell)
commands.outcome:
PASTE INTO FILE: 
#!/usr/bin/env bash
cat "$HOME/sw-notes/commands.txt"

-----------------------------
TRANSLATION: 
"Use Bash language terminal to run this script"
"Show my commands.txt  in the terminal"
---------------------------
CTL + O  , then ENTER to SAVE
CTL + X   ,   to exit.

commands.origin: Add executable permission to my script 'samnotes'
commands.purpose: Changes the permissions of your script to make it a castable spell when you type 'samnotes' into terminal.
commands.outcome: Tells Ubuntu : "Allow this script to be run like a command.”

commands.origin: Reload Users commands/settings for the current terminal
commands.purpose: This refreshes the system so it will recognize the new COMMAND phrase you have just programed.
commands.outcome: Nothing, Now you can cast your Spell.

lesson.exercise: In your terminal....  just type 'samnotes' .........  If your commands.txt appears,  you ARE NOW A SAMWIZARD GRADUATE!!!!!
lesson.paste_prompt: Optional: paste the Active line or write 'not installed yet'.

skills.title: Installing Apps & Managing Services
skills.summary: Learn how Ubuntu installs software, how to search for apps, how to install or remove them, and how to tell the difference between an installed program and a running service.

intro:
Ubuntu Has Software Repositories, it does not usually download apps from random websites. Instead, Ubuntu uses software sources called repositories. A repository is like Ubuntu’s pre-approved app warehouse.

When you install something with the command 'apt', Ubuntu checks its trusted software lists, downloads the package, installs it, and tracks it for future updates.
- Package....	A bundle of software Ubuntu can install
- App/program....	Something you can run, like btop or nano
- Service....	A background program that keeps running, like Samba, SSH, or Tailscale
- Repository....	A trusted software source Ubuntu downloads packages from

There are OTHER ways to install apps, just like you did with SamWizard.

Quick Comparison Table:

APT (apt): Installs native system packages. Updates maintained by the Linux distribution. Usually older, highly stable versions. Full access to your system.

SNAP (snap): Installs sandboxed, self-contained apps. Updates automated by the app developer. Bleeding-edge, latest versions. Isolated in a secure "sandbox".

CURL (curl): Downloads a single file or script from a URL. Manual Updates by you. High risk if you pipe an untrusted script to bash.

commands.purpose: btop is a system monitoring tool, like your 'task manager' in windows, cool app, good to have.  Great practice install, go for it.
commands.outcome: Prints device names, Tailscale IPs, and connection status.

commands.purpose: Open the app btop that you just installed.
commands.outcome: You'll see.

commands.purpose: This can be a LONG LONG list, try adding a 'grep' search word. 'grep' tells the system to look for specific text in it's search.
commands.outcome: apt list --installed | grep samwizard

commands.purpose: A package can be installed but not currently running. Installing software puts it on the server. Starting a service makes it actively run in the background.
commands.outcome:
Look for:
active (running)

commands.purpose: Make sure the services you want to be running, is.
commands.outcome:
List of active services. 
TRY: 
systemctl list-units --type=service

...for services , running or not.

commands.purpose: Tells Ubuntu to start SAMBA every time the system boots or reboot.
commands.outcome:
-sudo systemctl start smbd  - "Start Samba Now"
-sudo systemctl stop smbd - "Stop Samba Now"
-sudo systemctl restart smbd - "Restart Samba now"

lesson.exercise: install 'btop' and take it for a spin
